summary: Berry O'Kelly (c. 1861–1931) was a Black businessman, postmaster, and philanthropist in Raleigh, North Carolina, who developed commercial infrastructure in freedmen’s communities and co-founded the Berry O'Kelly School with Rosenwald funding.

claims.predicate: served as first postmaster of Method
co-founded Berry O'Kelly School with Rosenwald funding
claims.object: Berry O'Kelly Training School established in 1910 with support from Julius Rosenwald
claims.citationLabel: NC Department of Natural and Cultural Resources

claims.predicate: developed Black commercial district on East Hargett Street
claims.object: Acquired real estate and leased buildings to Black businesses on East Hargett Street, Raleigh

claims.predicate: served as vice-president of Mechanics and Farmers Bank Raleigh branch
claims.object: Branch manager and vice-president of Mechanics and Farmers Bank in Raleigh
claims.citationLabel: NC Department of Natural and Cultural Resources

claims.predicate: owned and operated multiple Black-owned businesses
claims.object: Owned Woods and O'Kelly general store, Raleigh Shoe Company, and Acme Realty Company
Donated 10 acres of land for the construction of the Berry O'Kelly School

historicalContext: During the rise of Jim Crow segregation in North Carolina, Black self-reliance and institution-building became critical survival strategies. O'Kelly’s development of Black-owned businesses and a vocational school in Method and Raleigh exemplified this resistance through economic and educational empowerment, supported by national networks like the Rosenwald Fund.

summary: John Patterson Green, born free in North Carolina, became a pioneering African American politician in Cleveland, Ohio. Elected Ohio’s first Black state senator in 1891, he championed Labor Day as a state holiday and served in both legislative chambers, reflecting Black political engagement during Reconstruction and its aftermath in the North.
keywords: Cuyahoga County Justice of the Peace

introduced legislation establishing Labor Day as a state holiday in Ohio
was elected Justice of the Peace in Cuyahoga County, Ohio
was born a free person of color in antebellum North Carolina
authored and self-published a pamphlet to fund his education
claims.object: Miscellaneous Subjects by a Self-Educated Colored Youth (1866)

claims.predicate: represented Cleveland and Cuyahoga County in the Ohio House of Representatives

historicalContext: Green's political career emerged during Reconstruction and its rollback, a period when Black men in Northern states like Ohio gained elected office despite rising segregation elsewhere. His success in a majority-white electorate illustrates how integrated Northern cities allowed limited but significant Black political advancement before Jim Crow's full entrenchment.
